title: A Comparison of Japan's New Digital Nomad Visa vs. the Business Manager Visa
primary_keyword: japan digital nomad visa vs business manager

author.name: Isabelle Rossi
author.bio: Having lived and worked in over 30 countries, Isabelle is a leading voice on the digital nomad movement. She covers everything from visa strategies and travel hacking to maintaining well-being on the road.

reviewedBy.name: Priya Singh, Esq.
reviewedBy.bio: Priya is an attorney specializing in international contract law for independent contractors. She ensures that the legal advice provided is accurate, actionable, and up-to-date with current regulations.
credentials: Member of the New York State Bar

key_takeaways: Align your visa choice with your business timeline: use the Digital Nomad visa for a six-month market test and the Business Manager visa to build a long-term, Japan-based enterprise.
While on the Digital Nomad visa, strictly avoid any work for or payment from Japanese entities to maintain compliance and prevent creating a taxable presence for your foreign company.
Commit to the Business Manager visa by incorporating a Japanese company with ¥5 million in capital, which establishes you and your business as Japanese tax residents.
Plan to leave Japan and re-apply from abroad when transitioning from a Digital Nomad to a Business Manager visa, as an in-country status change is generally not permitted.
summary: For solo professionals, choosing between Japan's new Digital Nomad and established Business Manager visas is a critical decision fraught with financial and operational risk. The article advises reframing the choice around your strategic timeline: use the Digital Nomad visa for a short-term "test drive" to validate the market, and select the Business Manager visa to build a long-term foundation with the legal authority to serve Japanese clients. This framework provides the clarity needed to mitigate risk, protect your business, and operate in Japan with confidence and control.
ai_overview_answer:
Choosing between Japan's Digital Nomad and Business Manager visa depends entirely on your business timeline and objectives. The Digital Nomad visa is for a short-term, six-month "test drive," allowing you to live in Japan while working remotely for non-Japanese clients. To qualify, you need a ¥10 million annual income and a passport from a treaty country, but you cannot earn income from Japanese sources. In contrast, the Business Manager visa is the required path for long-term establishment and growth within Japan.

This visa demands a greater commitment, requiring you to incorporate a Japanese company, invest at least ¥5 million in capital, and secure a physical office. By doing so, you gain the legal authority to serve Japanese clients and operate as a legitimate local business. A critical distinction is that Business Managers become Japanese tax residents, while Digital Nomads do not owe Japanese tax on their foreign income. Ultimately, the Business Manager visa is a renewable status that provides a direct pathway to permanent residency, whereas the Digital Nomad visa is a one-time, non-renewable stay.

itemListElement.name: A Comparison of Japan's New Digital Nomad Visa vs. the Business Manager Visa

@graph.name: How to Choose Between Japan's Digital Nomad and Business Manager Visas
@graph.description: A strategic guide for founders to mitigate risk and choose the right visa path for their 1, 3, and 5-year goals in Japan.

step.name: Define Your Mission: What's Your 1, 3, and 5-Year Japan Plan?
step.text: Begin by assessing your own timeline. Reframe the choice around your mission horizon: where do you see your business and life in six months, three years, and five years? This clarity helps select the right visa. The Digital Nomad visa is for a 6-month 'test drive,' the Business Manager visa is for a 3-year foundation, and it's also the path for 5+ year integration and permanent residency.

step.name: Execute the DN Visa Playbook: Your 6-Month Strategic 'Test Drive'
step.text: Treat the six-month period as an intelligence-gathering operation. In the first 30 days, focus on operational setup: open a bank account with a foreigner-friendly bank like Prestia SMBC Trust Bank, and secure short-term housing like a serviced apartment. Critically, stay within compliance rules by not working for any Japanese entity to avoid Permanent Establishment (PE) risk. You can bring your spouse and children with the proper documentation.

step.name: Execute the BM Visa Playbook: Build Your 'Business-of-One' for the Long Term
step.text: Commit to building a long-term foundation. Incorporate a Japanese company, typically a Godo Kaisha (GK), and invest the required ¥5 million in capital. Secure a compliant physical office, such as a private room in a serviced office. Embrace your new role as a Japanese tax resident, filing both corporate and personal taxes, which is crucial for visa renewal and eventual permanent residency.

step.name: Plan for 'What If' Scenarios and Contingencies
step.text: Understand how to pivot from a DN to a BM visa, which requires leaving Japan and re-applying from abroad. Adhere to a strict compliance checklist for each visa, including tax obligations and maintaining a physical office. Have a professional exit plan to properly dissolve your company and settle all affairs if you decide to leave Japan, ensuring no liabilities remain.

mainEntity.name: Can you switch from a Digital Nomad Visa to a Business Manager Visa?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, but it requires a strategic, two-step process. The DN visa is a temporary visitor status, meaning you generally cannot apply for a change to a long-term residency visa from within Japan. The proper method is to use your six months in Japan to prepare your Godo Kaisha and business plan. Once ready, you must leave Japan, apply for a Certificate of Eligibility (CoE) from abroad, and then re-enter once it's approved.

mainEntity.name: How do you open a bank account in Japan on the Digital Nomad Visa?

acceptedAnswer.text: While you receive a Residence Card (Zairyū Kādo) with the DN visa, many major banks have an internal policy requiring at least six months of residency before opening an account. This can create a challenge. Your best strategy is to target foreigner-friendly banks like Prestia SMBC Trust Bank or Sony Bank, which are known to be more flexible with new arrivals. For your six-month stay, also be prepared to rely on international multi-currency accounts and credit cards with no foreign transaction fees.

mainEntity.name: Can I work for Japanese clients on the Digital Nomad Visa?

acceptedAnswer.text: Absolutely not. This is the most critical compliance rule. The visa explicitly permits you to live in Japan while performing remote work for an employer or clients based outside of Japan. Engaging in any work for a Japanese company or receiving payment from a Japanese entity is a direct violation of your visa status.

mainEntity.name: What are the exact tax requirements for a US citizen on Japan's Digital Nomad Visa?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your U.S. tax obligations remain fully intact. As a U.S. citizen, you are taxed on your worldwide income. While in Japan on the six-month DN visa, you will not be considered a Japanese tax resident, so you won't owe Japanese income tax on your foreign earnings. However, you must still file your U.S. tax return. You likely will not qualify for the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE), which requires a much longer period abroad. You must also comply with FBAR (Foreign Bank Account Report) filing requirements if your foreign financial accounts exceed $10,000.

mainEntity.name: Is the Business Manager visa suitable for a solo freelancer or consultant?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, it is the standard and proper pathway for a solo professional who wants to establish a long-term business in Japan. The key is a shift in structure: you are not applying as a 'freelancer,' but as the director of your own Japanese company. By establishing a legal entity like a Godo Kaisha (GK) and investing the required ¥5 million in capital, you legitimize your 'Business-of-One' and meet the core requirements.

mainEntity.name: What are the consequences of overstaying the 6-month Digital Nomad Visa?

acceptedAnswer.text: The consequences are severe. Overstaying your visa, even by a single day, is a violation of Japan's Immigration Control Act. Penalties can include significant fines (up to ¥3 million), detention, and deportation. An overstay will also result in a ban on re-entering Japan for five to ten years, jeopardizing any future ambitions in the country.

body.content: Japan Is Open for Business. But Which Visa Protects *Yours*?

Japan has launched its Digital Nomad visa, creating a powerful new current of opportunity for professionals worldwide. For a sophisticated "Business-of-One" like you, however, this is not a simple travel choice. It is a critical business decision with long-term consequences for your financial health and professional trajectory.

Choosing the wrong path—the one that feels easiest today—can lead to operational nightmares, unforeseen tax traps, and a dead end for your ambitions. Standard visa comparisons lay out the facts but ignore the core anxiety that keeps a founder up at night: **How do I make this move without exposing my business, my assets, and my future to unnecessary risk?** They tell you *what* the visas are, but not *how* they protect you or where they leave you vulnerable.

This is not another "vs." article. This is a CEO's playbook for your expansion into Asia. We will move beyond a surface-level comparison of the Digital Nomad and Business Manager visas to provide a risk mitigation framework. This guide will help you choose the right path based on your 1, 3, and 5-year goals, giving you the clarity to build your future in Japan with confidence.
Before You Compare Visas, Define Your Mission: What's Your 1, 3, and 5-Year Japan Plan?

A strategic approach begins not with a visa application, but with a clear-eyed assessment of your own timeline. Instead of getting lost in a feature-by-feature comparison, let's reframe the choice around your mission horizon. Where do you see your business—and your life—in six months, three years, and five years? Your answer provides the clarity needed to select the right tool for the job.
This isn't just about working in Japan; it's about building a compliant, resilient, and intentional future. Let’s map your ambition to the correct visa framework.
The 6-Month "Test Drive": The Digital Nomad Visa

Think of the Digital Nomad (DN) visa as a strategic reconnaissance mission. It is the perfect instrument for a six-month tour to experience Japan with minimal commitment. Crucially, as your work is for entities outside Japan, you have no Japanese tax liability on your foreign income. This path is purpose-built for the professional who needs to test the market, understand the culture, and validate the lifestyle before committing to a deeper integration. It's a low-risk, high-information maneuver.
To qualify, you must meet two key conditions:

items: **Nationality:** You must hold a passport from one of the 49 countries that have a tax treaty with Japan.
**Income:** You must have a verifiable annual income of at least ¥10 million (approximately $66,000 USD).

body.content: This visa allows a single, non-renewable six-month stay. It is your opportunity to gather intelligence, build networks, and decide if Japan is the right long-term move—all without the heavy administrative lift of establishing a local company.
The 3-Year "Foundation": The Business Manager Visa for Solo Professionals

If your reconnaissance is complete and you're serious about Japan, this is your playbook. The Business Manager (BM) visa is the pathway for the established "Business-of-One" ready to build a real foundation. Many solo professionals are deterred by the requirement to establish a company with at least ¥5 million in capital. However, this is not an insurmountable obstacle; it's a structural one.

You meet this requirement by incorporating your solo business as a Japanese entity—most commonly a *Godo Kaisha* (GK), similar to a U.S. LLC. This structure allows you to be the sole investor and director, funding the ¥5 million capital yourself while maintaining complete control. By establishing a legal entity, you gain the critical ability to serve Japanese clients directly, open a corporate bank account, and operate as a legitimate local business. This is the definitive path for professionals who see Japan as a core part of their medium-term strategy.
The 5+ Year "Integration": The Business Manager Visa for Empire Builders

For those with a grander vision, the choice is unequivocal. The Business Manager visa is the only viable path for professionals who intend to put down permanent roots, scale operations, hire staff, and pursue long-term residency. This visa is your direct and renewable gateway to stability. After a period of continuous residence, typically ten years (though this can be shortened for highly skilled professionals), it opens the door to applying for Permanent Residency.

This makes the BM visa the foundational pillar for long-term immigration. It provides the legal structure necessary to hire employees, lease long-term office space, and fully integrate into the Japanese economy. If your 5-year plan involves more than just yourself—if it includes a team, a physical headquarters, and a life deeply embedded in Japan—then building your "Business-of-One" into a formal Japanese company isn't an option; it is the entire game.
The DN Visa Playbook: Your 6-Month Strategic "Test Drive"

Before you build an empire, you must survey the territory. Think of the next six months not as a working vacation, but as a focused intelligence-gathering operation. Your mission is to validate Japan as a market and a lifestyle, which requires a solid operational setup from day one.
Your First 30 Days: The Operational Checklist
A smooth landing is about anticipating needs and having the right documents ready.

items: **Banking:** While some major Japanese banks require a six-month residency period before opening an account, a few are more welcoming to new arrivals. Focus on banks like **Prestia SMBC Trust Bank** or **Sony Bank**, which have robust English support. You will arrive at a branch with your passport and your Residence Card (the *Zairyū Kādo* you receive at airport immigration). Having a Japanese phone number is also a standard requirement. This account is your first step to feeling financially settled.

**Housing:** Traditional apartment rentals are notoriously difficult for short-term stays, often requiring long contracts, a Japanese guarantor, and significant non-refundable deposits. Sidestep this system. Your strategy should be to secure a high-quality, **short-term serviced apartment** or a "monthly mansion." Platforms specializing in foreigner-friendly rentals allow you to book and sign your lease entirely online, often only requiring a copy of your passport. You arrive with a home ready, complete with furniture, utilities, and internet, allowing you to focus on your mission, not logistics.

body.content: The Compliance Tightrope: Staying Within the Rules

The freedom of the DN visa is balanced by one critical rule: you cannot perform work for or be paid by any Japanese entity. This seems simple, but the anxiety lies in the details, particularly around a concept called "Permanent Establishment" (PE) risk.

In essence, PE risk is the danger that your activities in Japan could create a taxable presence for your foreign company. If you, for example, have the authority to conclude contracts on behalf of your employer and do so from a fixed base in Japan (even a home office), you could inadvertently trigger Japanese corporate tax obligations for them.
To mitigate this risk, your actions must be clear:

items: **You are a visitor performing work for overseas entities.** You are not establishing a Japanese branch of your company.
**Do not engage in sales or contract negotiations with Japanese clients** on behalf of your foreign employer.
**Limit activities that could be seen as core to the business's revenue generation** within Japan. Your work should be directed outward.

body.content: Bringing Your Life with You

This reconnaissance mission doesn't have to be a solo affair. The visa allows your legally married spouse and dependent children to join you, a significant advantage for evaluating Japan as a long-term home for your family.
To ensure they can join you seamlessly, prepare official documentation proving your relationship:

items: **For your spouse:** A government-issued marriage certificate.
**For your children:** Their official birth certificates.

body.content: Alongside their own application forms and passports, you must also provide a copy of your passport and proof that your private health insurance covers them for the duration of your stay.
The BM Visa Playbook: Building Your "Business-of-One" for the Long Term

A strategic reconnaissance mission is one thing; building a legitimate, long-term foundation is another. This requires shifting from the temporary "test drive" of the DN visa to the permanent architecture of the Business Manager visa. This path demands a greater commitment, but it grants you what the DN visa never can: the legal authority to serve Japanese clients, generate domestic revenue, and build a lasting enterprise.
From Zero to Incorporation: The Godo Kaisha (GK) Strategy

The most significant mental hurdle for the solo professional is often the visa's requirement to establish a company with a capital of at least ¥5 million. This figure is not a fee; it is an investment in your own enterprise. The most direct way to structure this is by creating a *Godo Kaisha* (GK), the Japanese equivalent of an LLC.
The GK structure is ideal for a "Business-of-One" for several reasons:

items: **Complete Control:** You can be the sole investor and the sole director (termed a Representative Member). Your ¥5 million investment goes directly into a company you own and control 100%.
**Simplicity and Cost:** Compared to a *Kabushiki Kaisha* (KK), or joint-stock company, a GK is simpler and less expensive to establish, as its articles of incorporation do not need to be notarized.
**Visa Legitimacy:** A properly capitalized GK is viewed by immigration authorities as a legitimate and sustainable business entity, satisfying a core requirement.

body.content: Securing Your Capital & Office (Without Breaking the Bank)
With the corporate structure decided, your next focus is on two practical pillars: the capital and the office.

**1. The Capital:** Proving the legitimacy of your ¥5 million is non-negotiable. Immigration officials scrutinize the source of funds to prevent fraud. You must provide a clear, documented paper trail showing how you accumulated the money. The process involves transferring the full amount into a Japanese bank account and obtaining a certificate of deposit, which becomes a key part of your application.

**2. The Office:** The BM visa requires a physical office space. Standard virtual offices or shared co-working desks are generally not accepted, as immigration needs to see a dedicated, independent space for your business. A cost-effective and compliant solution is to rent a private, lockable room within a serviced office or co-working facility. This provides a legitimate lease agreement in your company's name and a physical space to present to authorities, satisfying the requirement without massive overhead.
Embracing Your Role as a Japanese Tax Resident

This is the most critical mindset shift. Unlike the DN visa, the BM visa establishes you and your company as Japanese tax residents. This is not a burden; it is the cornerstone of your integration and legitimacy.
Your obligations are clear:

items: **Corporate Taxes:** Your *Godo Kaisha* will be liable for Japanese corporate taxes on its profits.
**Personal Income Tax:** You will pay personal income tax in Japan on the salary you draw from your company.

body.content: Properly filing these taxes is not just a legal requirement; it is your direct path toward long-term stability. Consistent tax compliance is a key factor immigration authorities review when you apply to renew your visa and, eventually, for Permanent Residency.

Navigating this new landscape requires diligence. As bilingual immigration lawyer Saki Nakahara of Small Seasons & Co. advises, a common failure point is a lack of ongoing communication. "I think the challenge for foreigners when living in Japan, in terms of immigration, is not knowing when to communicate with the immigration," she notes. "People get their visa and... can just live in Japan legally until the date expires. But actually, depending on what you do... maybe you need to get special permission." This underscores the importance of viewing compliance not as a one-time event, but as a continuous process of responsible business ownership.
"What If" Scenarios: A CEO's Guide to Contingency Planning

Viewing your visa as a continuous process of responsible ownership separates a smooth long-term stay from a turbulent one. Every strategic decision requires a contingency plan, a clear understanding of the variables, and a plan for a professional exit.
The Pivot Strategy: Transitioning from "Test Drive" to "Foundation"

You've completed your six-month reconnaissance on the DN visa and are ready to commit. Can you switch to the BM visa? Yes, but it requires a strategic exit and re-entry. The DN visa generally does not permit an in-country change to a long-term work or business visa.
This requires a forward-thinking approach. The smart strategy is to use your time in Japan to lay the groundwork.

items: **Prepare While Onshore:** During your six-month stay, you can legally begin establishing your *Godo Kaisha*, securing your ¥5 million capital, leasing a compliant office, and preparing your business plan.

**Apply from Offshore:** Once your corporate entity is established and all documents are prepared, you must leave Japan. You will then apply for a Certificate of Eligibility (CoE) from your home country or a third country.

**Return and Launch:** After the CoE is approved (a process that can take several months), you will have the visa stamped into your passport at a Japanese embassy or consulate and can then re-enter Japan as a resident business manager.

body.content: The Compliance Guardrails Checklist
These are the non-negotiable rules for operating with confidence under each visa.

rows: **Track your days meticulously.** The six-month limit is firm.
**Perform any work for or receive payment from a Japanese company.** This is a bright-line rule you cannot cross.
**For U.S. Citizens: Plan for your tax obligations.** Your filing requirements do not stop just because you are abroad.
**Forget your FBAR obligations.** If the aggregate value of your foreign accounts exceeds $10,000 at any point, you must file.
**File your corporate and personal taxes in Japan on time.** This is the bedrock of your legitimacy for visa renewal.
**Mix personal and business funds.** Maintain the legal separation of your GK to protect yourself and demonstrate professionalism.
**Maintain your physical office registration.** Your registered office is a core visa requirement that must be kept current.
**Assume your initial business plan is static.** Keep records that show your business is active and evolving.

body.content: For American professionals, overlooking U.S. tax obligations is a particularly common and costly mistake. As Nicolas Castillo, Founder of Rook International CPAs and Advisors, warns, "The biggest mistake that I see with people moving anywhere outside of the US is they think, 'Hey, I've been outside of the US, I don't have to file a tax return anymore...' And that's really a big mistake." This highlights the critical need for proactive tax planning, even on a short-term visa.
The Professional Exit Plan
A clean exit is as important as a clean entry. Whether your plans change or your mission is complete, winding down your affairs properly ensures you leave no liabilities behind.

items:
**For Digital Nomad Visa Holders:**
    *   Settle all outstanding bills (rent, utilities, phone).
    *   Close any Japanese bank accounts.
    *   Keep a record of your departure date to prove you complied with the six-month limit.

**For Business Manager Visa Holders:**
    *   **Formal Dissolution:** Closing a *Godo Kaisha* is a formal legal procedure involving a resolution to dissolve, appointment of a liquidator, and registration with the Legal Affairs Bureau.
    *   **Creditor Notification:** You must provide public notice and a period of at least two months for any potential creditors to make claims.
    *   **Tax Clearance:** You must file final corporate tax returns and notify tax authorities of the company's dissolution.
    *   **Deregistration:** After all assets are distributed and taxes are paid, you must file for the registration of liquidation completion, at which point your company officially ceases to exist.

body.content: Managing these "what if" scenarios is the ultimate expression of control, ensuring your Japanese venture is executed with precision from start to finish.
Frequently Asked Questions
Executing your plan with precision also means having clear answers to the most common questions.

items:
**Can you switch from a Digital Nomad Visa to a Business Manager Visa?**
    Yes, but it requires a strategic, two-step process. The DN visa is a temporary visitor status, meaning you generally cannot apply for a change to a long-term residency visa from within Japan. The proper method is to use your six months in Japan to prepare your *Godo Kaisha* and business plan. Once ready, you must leave Japan, apply for a Certificate of Eligibility (CoE) from abroad, and then re-enter once it's approved.

**How do you open a bank account in Japan on the Digital Nomad Visa?**
    While you receive a Residence Card (*Zairyū Kādo*) with the DN visa, many major banks have an internal policy requiring at least six months of residency before opening an account. This can create a challenge. Your best strategy is to target foreigner-friendly banks like Prestia SMBC Trust Bank or Sony Bank, which are known to be more flexible with new arrivals. For your six-month stay, also be prepared to rely on international multi-currency accounts and credit cards with no foreign transaction fees.

**Can I work for Japanese clients on the Digital Nomad Visa?**
    Absolutely not. This is the most critical compliance rule. The visa explicitly permits you to live in Japan while performing remote work for an employer or clients based **outside** of Japan. Engaging in any work for a Japanese company or receiving payment from a Japanese entity is a direct violation of your visa status.

**What are the exact tax requirements for a US citizen on Japan's Digital Nomad Visa?**
    Your U.S. tax obligations remain fully intact. As a U.S. citizen, you are taxed on your worldwide income. While in Japan on the six-month DN visa, you will not be considered a Japanese tax resident, so you won't owe Japanese income tax on your foreign earnings. However, you must still file your U.S. tax return. You likely will not qualify for the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE), which requires a much longer period abroad. You must also comply with FBAR (Foreign Bank Account Report) filing requirements if your foreign financial accounts exceed $10,000.

**Is the Business Manager visa suitable for a solo freelancer or consultant?**
    Yes, it is the standard and proper pathway for a solo professional who wants to establish a long-term business in Japan. The key is a shift in structure: you are not applying as a "freelancer," but as the director of your own Japanese company. By establishing a legal entity like a *Godo Kaisha* (GK) and investing the required ¥5 million in capital, you legitimize your "Business-of-One" and meet the core requirements.

**What are the consequences of overstaying the 6-month Digital Nomad Visa?**
    The consequences are severe. Overstaying your visa, even by a single day, is a violation of Japan's Immigration Control Act. Penalties can include significant fines (up to ¥3 million), detention, and deportation. An overstay will also result in a ban on re-entering Japan for five to ten years, jeopardizing any future ambitions in the country.

body.content: The Final Verdict: Choosing Control Over Chance

The decision between the Digital Nomad and Business Manager visa is not a simple pros-and-cons list. It is a strategic inflection point that defines your future in the country. One path offers a taste; the other provides the tools to build a permanent foundation.

The **Digital Nomad visa** is your six-month, low-risk reconnaissance mission. It is a powerful tool for discovery, allowing you to validate your market and lifestyle fit without the complexities of Japanese tax residency or corporate formation. You operate with your existing foreign business, serving only non-Japanese clients. This is the path of **calculated exploration**, designed to answer one critical question: "Is Japan the right long-term move for my business and my life?"

Conversely, the **Business Manager visa** is the path of **deliberate construction**. It is the only viable framework for establishing a legitimate, long-term "Business-of-One" with the authority to operate within the Japanese economy. This route requires a significant commitment—incorporating a Japanese entity and investing capital—but the return is unparalleled **control**. You gain the legal standing to serve Japanese clients, hire staff, and fully integrate into the commercial landscape. More importantly, this visa is a renewable instrument that serves as your direct pathway to permanent residency. It transforms your status from a temporary guest into a recognized business leader.
To clarify this strategic choice, consider the core mission of each visa:

Ultimately, by aligning your visa choice with your defined business mission, you move from being a hopeful visitor to being the CEO of your life in Japan. You are consciously choosing between a short-term tour of duty and a long-term campaign. This isn't about chance. It is about seizing control of your professional destiny with clarity and purpose.
